1:1: The months of the Hebrew year follow the moon — each month begins when the moon renews itself. However, the years follow the sun, so that holidays always fall in the same season. God showed Moses the form of the new moon and told him: when you see the moon like this — sanctify it.
1:2: A solar year is about 11 days longer than a lunar year (12 lunar months). When this gap accumulates to about 30 days, an extra month is added to the year (a second Adar), making it 13 months. Thanks to this addition, Passover always falls in spring and Sukkot in autumn.
1:3: The moon disappears from the sky each month for about two days — one day before it aligns with the sun and one day after. When the moon reappears as a thin crescent in the western sky at evening, this signals the start of a new month.
1:4: A month in which the moon is sighted on the night of the thirtieth (meaning the previous month was 29 days) is called a "deficient month." A month in which the moon is not sighted on the night of the thirtieth and the month lasts 30 days is called a "full month" or "extended."
1:5: Determining the new month based on sighting the moon is not left to every individual, unlike Shabbat where everyone counts six days and rests on the seventh. Only the beit din (court) has the authority to sanctify the month and determine when it begins.
1:6: The beit din would calculate in advance, like astronomers, whether the moon was expected to be visible on the night of the thirtieth. If they calculated that it could not possibly be seen, they would not wait for witnesses that day. If they calculated that it could be seen, they would wait for witnesses to come and testify.
1:7: It is a positive commandment from the Torah for the beit din to calculate and determine whether the moon will be visible, to examine the witnesses, to sanctify the month, and to send messengers to inform all of Israel on which day the new month was established.
1:8: Establishing months and intercalating years is done only in the Land of Israel, as it says, "For out of Zion shall go forth Torah." If there is no ordained sage in the Land of Israel, it is possible to establish them outside the Land under special conditions. But if there is a Sanhedrin in the Land of Israel, only it may decide.
2:1: Only two valid witnesses may testify about sighting the new moon. Women, slaves, and those disqualified from testimony are not valid for this purpose. A father and son who both saw the moon may both go to the beit din, since one of them can join with another witness.
2:2: Strictly speaking, the testimony for the new month does not require excessive scrutiny — even if the beit din sanctified the month based on witnesses who were later found to be liars, the month remains sanctified after the fact. Therefore, originally they accepted testimony from any Jew, until the Sadducees caused problems and they enacted that testimony be accepted only from known individuals.
2:3: If the beit din does not recognize the witnesses who saw the moon, the people of the city where the moon was sighted send character witnesses along with the sighting witnesses. The character witnesses confirm to the beit din that the witnesses are valid and trustworthy.
2:4: The beit din calculates the moon's position in advance — in which direction it will appear, how wide it will be, and which way its horns will point. When the witnesses come, the beit din checks whether their testimony matches the calculation. If it matches, they accept it; if it contradicts, they investigate further.
2:5: If the witnesses said they saw the moon in water (a reflection), through clouds, or through glass — this is not a valid sighting and the month is not sanctified based on it. However, if one witness says he saw it high and another says low, their testimony is still valid.
2:6: Witnesses who said they saw the moon without intending to look, and after concentrating they could not see it again — their testimony is not accepted, because perhaps clouds appeared to look like the moon. But witnesses who saw it intentionally and then the moon disappeared — their testimony is valid.
2:7: The procedure for receiving testimony: anyone who saw the moon comes to the beit din. The beit din gathers them all in one place and prepares a large meal for them, to encourage people to come. Afterward, they examine the pair that arrived first.
2:8: After the testimony is examined and approved, the head of the beit din proclaims "Mekudash!" (sanctified!) and all the people respond after him "Mekudash! Mekudash!" The sanctification of the month requires at least three judges, and is done only on the thirtieth day — not at night.
2:9: If the beit din themselves saw the moon at the end of the twenty-ninth day before the stars came out, they may say "Mekudash" immediately. If they saw it on the night of the thirtieth (after the stars came out), they do not sanctify that night, but wait until the next day and sanctify it during the day.
2:10: If the beit din sanctified the month — whether they erred, were misled by false witnesses, or were coerced — the month is sanctified and cannot be reversed. All of Israel must set the holidays according to the day the beit din established, even if it is known that there was an error.
3:1: Witnesses who saw the new moon must travel to testify before the beit din. If the distance between them and the beit din is a day-and-night's journey or less, they go. If it is more than that, they do not go, since their testimony would not arrive in time.
3:2: Witnesses who sighted the moon travel to the beit din even on Shabbat, because establishing the holidays overrides Shabbat. Therefore, violating Shabbat is permitted for the purpose of moon testimony for the six months in which holidays occur.
3:3: Just as the sighting witnesses themselves may violate Shabbat, so too may the character witnesses who accompany them — if the beit din does not recognize the sighting witnesses. Even a single person who testifies to the witnesses' validity may violate Shabbat to accompany them.
3:4: If the witness who saw the moon is ill, he may be carried on a donkey on Shabbat, or even on a bed. If there is danger on the road, they may carry weapons. If the journey is long, they may take food. All of this is permitted on Shabbat for the purpose of moon testimony.
3:5: Originally, they accepted moon testimony throughout the entire thirtieth day. Once the witnesses were delayed until the afternoon, and a problem arose in the Temple — they did not know whether to offer the Musaf sacrifice for the new month. Therefore, they enacted that witnesses be accepted only until the Minchah (afternoon) time.
3:6: If the time of Minchah arrived and no witnesses came, they offer the afternoon Tamid sacrifice as usual. If witnesses arrived after Minchah, that day is treated as holy and the next day as well, and the Musaf sacrifice is offered the next day. This is because the month is not sanctified from Minchah onward.
3:7: When no witnesses came on the thirtieth day and the beit din extended the month (making it 30 days), the beit din ascends to a special place on the thirty-first day (which is the new month) and holds a meal. They do not go up at night, only in the morning.
3:8: In Temple times, when the beit din sanctified the month, they would light signal fires on mountaintops to announce it to distant places. When the Kutim (Sadducees) caused problems by lighting false signals, they enacted that messengers be sent instead.
3:9: Messengers were sent to announce the sanctification of the month for six months: Nisan (for Passover), Av (for the fast), Elul (for Rosh Hashanah), Tishrei (for Sukkot and Yom Kippur), Kislev (for Hanukkah), and Adar (for Purim).
3:10: The messengers for Nisan and Tishrei depart only on the day of the new month itself, after sunrise, once they have heard the beit din proclaim "Mekudash." Messengers for the other months may depart even on the eve of the new month if they already heard that it was sanctified.
3:11: Every place the messengers reached observed the holidays for one day as written in the Torah. Distant places that the messengers did not reach observed two days out of doubt, since they did not know on which day the beit din sanctified the month.
3:12: There are places that the messengers for Nisan reached but not the messengers for Tishrei. Strictly speaking, they should have observed Passover for one day and Sukkot for two. However, it was enacted that they observe two days for all holidays, so as not to differentiate between one holiday and another.
3:13: The difference between the messengers for Nisan and those for Tishrei is two days: the Tishrei messengers cannot travel on the first of Tishrei (Rosh Hashanah) or on the tenth (Yom Kippur), so they reach a shorter distance.
3:14: The messengers do not need to be two — even a single messenger is trusted. And it need not even be an official messenger — if a regular merchant came and said he heard from the beit din that they sanctified on a certain day, he is relied upon.
3:15: If the beit din sat throughout the thirtieth day and no witnesses came, and they rose in the morning and extended the month, and after several days distant witnesses came and testified that they saw the moon — the beit din examines their testimony. If their testimony cannot be accepted, the month remains extended.
3:16: If the testimony of the late witnesses passes examination, the witnesses are known and valid, and their testimony aligns with the calculation — the beit din sanctifies retroactively, and they count the month from the thirtieth day on which the moon was sighted.
3:17: If the beit din needs to keep the month extended (30 days) even after witnesses came — they are permitted to do so. This is called "extending the month for a need." Some sages disagree and hold that the month must be sanctified whenever valid witnesses come.
3:18: The Rambam holds that this dispute (whether the month may be extended for a need) applies only to months that have no holiday (not Nisan or Tishrei), or when the witnesses came after the holiday had passed. If the witnesses came before the holiday, their testimony must be accepted.
3:19: Pressure is applied to witnesses whose testimony was undermined so that they correct it and the month is sanctified on time, to prevent unnecessary extension. Similarly, if witnesses came to impeach the sighting witnesses, they are not heard, so as not to invalidate a month that was already sanctified.
4:1: A leap year is a year in which a thirteenth month is added. The added month is always Adar — resulting in a first Adar and a second Adar. The reason: so that Passover always falls in spring, as the Torah commands, "Observe the month of Aviv (spring)."
4:2: Three signs determine whether the year should be intercalated: (1) the Tekufah of Nisan (the vernal equinox) — if it would fall after the sixteenth of Nisan, they intercalate; (2) the barley has not yet ripened; (3) the fruit trees have not yet blossomed. The first sign (the Tekufah) alone is sufficient to intercalate.
4:3: If the beit din saw that the barley had not yet ripened and the fruit trees had not yet blossomed, they may rely on these two signs and intercalate. Even if the Tekufah did not require intercalation, these two natural signs are sufficient.
4:4: Checking the ripeness of the barley is done in three regions of the Land of Israel: Judea, Transjordan, and the Galilee. If the barley ripened in two of them, the year is not intercalated. If it ripened in only one and not the other two, the year is intercalated.
4:5: There are additional reasons for which the beit din may intercalate the year out of necessity: roads that have not been repaired and are impassable for pilgrimage, bridges that have been destroyed, Passover ovens that have been destroyed, or Jewish communities from the Diaspora that have already set out on their journey but have not yet arrived.
4:6: The year is not intercalated because of snow, cold, Jewish communities that have not yet departed from their place, or ritual impurity affecting the majority of the public. Nor is it intercalated in order to postpone the Sabbatical year or to gain extra time.
4:7: There are factors that by themselves are not sufficient grounds for intercalation, but serve as supporting evidence when the year already needs intercalation for a primary reason. For example: lambs and kids that are still too young, pigeons that are still too tender, or insufficient rainfall.
4:8: How are these supporting factors used? They say: "The year requires intercalation because the Tekufah is late, or because the barley and fruit trees have not matured — and additionally, the lambs are small and the pigeons are tender." That is, the secondary factors reinforce the decision.
4:9: Intercalation of the year is carried out only by judges who were specifically invited for this purpose. The head of the great beit din tells the members of the Sanhedrin: assemble at a certain place to deliberate whether the year requires intercalation. Anyone who was not invited may not participate in the decision.
4:10: If two judges say the year should be intercalated and three say it should not, the majority rules and it is not intercalated. If three say yes and two say no, two more judges from among the invitees are added, they deliberate as seven, and the majority decides.
4:11: Neither a king nor a High Priest is seated on the intercalation committee. A king — lest he wish to intercalate for the needs of his army. A High Priest — lest he not wish to intercalate, since on Yom Kippur he immerses in cold water, and an extra month lengthens the winter.
4:12: If the head of the beit din (the Nasi) is away on a distant journey, the year is intercalated only on condition that the Nasi agrees. If he agrees, it is intercalated; if not, it is not. The year is never intercalated two years in a row (three consecutive leap years never occur).
4:13: The beit din has the authority to calculate in advance which year will be a leap year, even several years ahead. However, they do not officially proclaim the intercalation until after Rosh Hashanah of that year, not before.
4:14: If the thirtieth of Adar arrives and the year has still not been intercalated, it is too late — because that day is fit to be the first of Nisan. However, if they intercalated on the thirtieth day itself (before it was established as the new month), the intercalation is valid.
4:15: The year is not intercalated in a year of famine, because everyone rushes to the granary to eat and they cannot be prohibited from eating new grain for an extended period. Nor is it intercalated in a Sabbatical year, because there are no aftergrowths and they would have nothing to offer as the Omer.
4:16: The Rambam explains that the prohibition against intercalating in a famine year or Sabbatical year applies only when the intercalation is for secondary reasons (roads, bridges). But if the year requires intercalation for a primary reason (Tekufah or the state of the crops), it is intercalated even in famine and Sabbatical years.
4:17: When the beit din intercalates the year, they write letters to all distant places informing them of the intercalation and its reason. The letters are written in the name of the Nasi, and they detail the reason — for example: the lambs are small, the pigeons are tender, and the spring has not yet arrived.
5:1: Everything stated about sanctifying the month based on sighting the moon and intercalating the year was done only by the Sanhedrin in the Land of Israel, or by an ordained beit din that received authorization from the Sanhedrin. This is the exclusive authority for establishing the calendar.
5:2: This is a halachah transmitted to Moses at Sinai: when there is a Sanhedrin, the months are established based on sighting the moon. When there is no Sanhedrin, they are established based on a fixed calculation (the calendar we use today). Whether the moon is visible or not is irrelevant; the calculation governs.
5:3: When did all of Israel begin using the fixed calculation? At the end of the Talmudic era, when the Land of Israel was laid waste and no permanent beit din remained. But during the periods of the Mishnah and Talmud, the months were still established based on actual sighting.
5:4: When the Sanhedrin was active and established months based on sighting: the residents of the Land of Israel and places reached by the Tishrei messengers observed each holiday for one day. Distant places not reached by the messengers observed two days out of doubt.
5:5: In the present era when there is no Sanhedrin and the calendar is fixed by calculation, strictly speaking everyone should observe only one day of Yom Tov (since everyone knows when the month begins). However, the sages enacted that those in the Diaspora continue to observe two days, following the custom of their ancestors.
5:6: Therefore, every place that the Tishrei messengers would not have reached when they used to go out observes two days even in the present era, just as they practiced when months were established by sighting. And the residents of the Land of Israel observe one day as always.
5:7: Rosh Hashanah is a special case: even when months were established by sighting, most residents of the Land of Israel observed it for two days out of doubt, because they did not know exactly on which day the beit din sanctified the month — since Rosh Hashanah falls on the first of Tishrei itself.
5:8: Even in Jerusalem itself, the seat of the beit din, Rosh Hashanah was sometimes observed for two days — when witnesses did not come on the thirtieth day. Therefore, Rosh Hashanah is always two days, even in the Land of Israel, even in the present era.
5:9: Observing one or two days of Yom Tov does not depend solely on distance from Jerusalem. For example, a place only five days away — if the custom there was to observe two days, they continue to do so. The historical custom is what determines the practice.
5:10: If it depended only on distance, all the residents of Egypt would observe one day, since from Jerusalem to Egypt via Ashkelon is only eight days — enough time for the Tishrei messengers to arrive. Yet in practice, the custom in Egypt was two days.
5:11: The rule: every place more than ten days' journey from Jerusalem always observes two days, as was their longstanding custom. Because the messengers of no month could have reached them in time.
5:12: A place that is ten days or fewer from Jerusalem, located in Syria or outside the Land of Israel, with no established custom — observes two days, following the practice of most of the world. The same applies to a new city in the Land of Israel that has no established custom.
5:13: The calculation that each person makes in his own city in the present era — we do not establish the calendar based on it. What matters is that the people of the Land of Israel calculated and sanctified, and we rely on them. Without the people of the Land of Israel, we have no calendar — for authority goes forth only from there.
